**Li Jinyu** ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 李金羽; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Lǐ Jīnyǔ*; born 6 July 1977) is a Chinese former international football player who is currently the head coach of [Liaoning Tieren](/wiki/Liaoning_Tieren_F.C. "Liaoning Tieren F.C.").

As a player he represented [Liaoning Whowin](/wiki/Liaoning_Whowin "Liaoning Whowin") and [Shandong Luneng](/wiki/Shandong_Luneng_Taishan_F.C. "Shandong Luneng Taishan F.C.") in the [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League") and spent one season on loan at French club [AS Nancy](/wiki/AS_Nancy "AS Nancy"). He is also currently the top goalscorer in Chinese professional league history and won the Golden Boot Award three times in the process.

## Club career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_Jinyu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Club career")]

One of the premier strikers in China[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], Li Jinyu earned his reputation early when he was the stars of the Chinese youth team that studied in Brazil for a training programme sponsored by [Shenzhen Jianlibao](/wiki/Shenzhen_Shangqingyin "Shenzhen Shangqingyin"). This then saw him called up to the [Chinese under-20 national team](/wiki/China_national_under-20_football_team "China national under-20 football team") and gave him a chance to play in the [1997 FIFA World Youth Championship](/wiki/1997_FIFA_World_Youth_Championship "1997 FIFA World Youth Championship") which attracted the interests of French side [AS Nancy](/wiki/AS_Nancy "AS Nancy") who acquired him on loan for one season.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Li's time at AS Nancy was not successful and he would return to China to sign with [Liaoning Whowin](/wiki/Liaoning_Whowin "Liaoning Whowin"), immediately showing his potential that was missing at AS Nancy when he became an integral member of the team that narrowly lost out on the league title in 1999.[[2]](#cite_note-2) While he would personally win his first Golden Boot award in 2002, he did not win any trophies with his club Liaoning during his time with them.

At the beginning of the 2004 league season, [Shandong Luneng](/wiki/Shandong_Luneng "Shandong Luneng") were willing to pay 4,900,000 yuan to make him the most expensive signing in Chinese football transfer history at the time. He would immediately become an integral member of the team and would repay them by helping them win the [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup") and [Chinese Super League Cup](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League_Cup "Chinese Super League Cup") at the end of the season. The 2006 league season would see him reach his peak when he won the league title, the Chinese FA Cup, and the Golden Boot award with the team. The 2007 league season would see him officially become the most prolific player within the league when he broke [Hao Haidong](/wiki/Hao_Haidong "Hao Haidong")'s goal record, however he could not aid Shandong to another league title.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Despite his tradition in scoring, his prolific goalscoring dropped during the 2008 league season when he only scored six goals, nevertheless he was still able to win another league title.[[4]](#cite_note-4) By the 2010 league season, Li would become a fringe player within the team, however he would still go on to win the league title once more and decided to retire at the end of the season.

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_Jinyu&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: International career")]

After playing in the [1997 FIFA World Youth Championship](/wiki/1997_FIFA_World_Youth_Championship "1997 FIFA World Youth Championship") for the [Chinese under-20 national team](/wiki/China_national_under-20_football_team "China national under-20 football team"), Li would immediately move to the senior team and would make his debut in a friendly against the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") in a 1-1 draw on 1 February 1997.[[5]](#cite_note-5) This then saw him become a regular with the [Chinese national team](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") and saw him play in several unsuccessful World Cup qualifiers. His biggest achievement came in the [2004 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2004_AFC_Asian_Cup "2004 AFC Asian Cup") where he was an integral member of the team that was runners-up in the tournament. When China qualified for the [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup"), then manager [Zhu Guanghu](/wiki/Zhu_Guanghu "Zhu Guanghu") dropped him from the squad to take part in the tournament because he believed that his performances were not convincing enough to be included in the squad.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Management career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_Jinyu&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Management career")]

Li started his managerial career by becoming the assistant coach of the [Chinese women's national team](/wiki/China_women%27s_national_football_team "China women's national football team") and [Shenyang Shenbei](/wiki/Shenyang_Shenbei "Shenyang Shenbei"). On 13 May 2013, he was appointed as the new manager of [China League One](/wiki/China_League_One "China League One") side [Shenyang Shenbei](/wiki/Shenyang_Shenbei "Shenyang Shenbei"), replacing [Liu Zhicai](/wiki/Liu_Zhicai "Liu Zhicai") who was sacked from the club on the same day after a string of poor results.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
